title: Tailwind v4 duration-token namespace mismatch (--duration-* vs --transition-duration-*)
description:
T03 ported --duration-fast/--duration-base into globals.css as plain CSS vars, but Tailwind v4's duration-* utilities are keyed off the --transition-duration-* namespace. As a result every duration-fast/duration-base class app-wide (button.tsx, switch.tsx, mobile-nav, possibly more) was silently emitting no utility and falling back to the default 150ms — the design's base(220ms)/fast(150ms) motion tokens were never actually applied as utilities.

The sidebar-fix agent worked around it locally (mobile-nav uses arbitrary [transition-duration:var(--dur-base)]), but the systemic fix is a one-line rename in globals.css: --duration-fast → --transition-duration-fast, --duration-base → --transition-duration-base (verify the exact Tailwind v4 namespace against the installed version via context7 first).

Verify against: docs/design_prototype (motion tokens), web/app/globals.css:348-350, and grep all duration-fast/duration-base usages. After the rename, audit that no transition silently changed timing in a regressions-introducing way (some may have been accidentally-correct at 150ms and should explicitly become duration-fast).
notes: ARTIFACT SOURCE OF TRUTH: /home/kenan/.traycer/epics/8b2235d5-8943-4f6d-a61e-3b671836217a/artifacts/rebuild-tech-plan/tickets/duration-token-namespace-repair/index.md. Status: in_progress pending fresh review. Corrected evidence from installed Tailwind 4.3.2 proves --transition-duration-* is the named utility namespace; Opus implemented the two-file repair and all implementation gates passed. Beads is a downstream mirror only.
close_reason: Completed after fresh closure review found no issues. Independently verified Tailwind 4.3.2 namespace/compile output, production CSS, 150ms scrim/220ms drawer, reduced motion, typecheck/lint/format, 60 focused Vitest and 24 Playwright.

title: T03 token collision: --on-ink == --chrome in dark theme (#0f1319)
description: During T08 cold-review fixes, the implementer found that in dark theme the design-system tokens --on-ink and --chrome are both #0f1319, so text-on-ink on a chrome/ink background renders invisible (1:1 contrast) in dark mode. T08 worked around it locally by using text-onbrand (#fff in both themes) for chrome controls, but the underlying token collision remains and will bite any chrome surface using text-on-ink in dark theme (incl. downstream editors T09-T15). Fix: give --on-ink a value in dark theme that actually contrasts with --chrome, or clarify the intended semantics of on-ink vs onbrand for chrome surfaces. Verify in web/app/globals.css.
close_reason: Fixed: .dark --on-ink #0f1319 -> #ffffff (no longer inverts, since ink/chrome bar is dark in both themes). Retired T08's onbrand workaround — chrome surfaces (top-bar, undo/redo, mobile-nav trigger) now use the correct text-on-ink token. Verified: typecheck 0, vitest 302 passed, prod build 17/17, oxlint/oxfmt clean; contrast-neutral vs prior onbrand (#fff both themes).

title: Ask how to set cors allowed domain ?
description: can we set it via env ? since env already sets the api domain
notes:
RESOLVED via the BFF architecture (DL11 D1 / tech-plan §3, ticket T06).

Q: can we set the CORS allowed domain via env, since env already sets the api domain?

A: You don't need to. Two parts:
1. NORMAL OPERATION — no CORS config needed. With the Next same-origin BFF, the browser only ever calls /api/* on the Next origin; the Next server calls the backend server-side inside the Docker network. The browser therefore NEVER triggers the backend's CORS allow-list, so the backend CORS domain is irrelevant to the deployed app. cloudflared fronts 'web'; 'web'->'backend' is server-side.
2. TODAY IT IS NOT ENV-DRIVEN — the backend CORS is a hardcoded regex in serve.py (allow_origin_regex = localhost | *.nursescheduling.org, C2 §Conformance). It is not read from env. The API domain the frontend uses is env (BACKEND_API_URL, server-side) — a different thing from the backend's CORS allow-list.
3. IF direct browser->backend access is ever wanted (bypassing the BFF) from a custom domain, making the CORS origin env-configurable would be a small backend config adaptation (same spirit as DL11 D2's version-fn change) — out of scope now; not required for the BFF deploy.
close_reason: Answered: BFF makes backend CORS config unnecessary for normal operation (same-origin); backend CORS is a hardcoded regex, not env — env only sets the server-side BACKEND_API_URL. See notes + DL11 D1 / T06.

title: Investigate: instrumentation.subprocess.test.ts valid-config case fails EVEN serially (hardcoded port 34572 not serviceable)
description:
OBSERVED (2026-07-17, across multiple T12 M1 gate runs): the vitest 'standalone server config fail-fast (subprocess) > binds and becomes serviceable when config is valid' case (web/instrumentation.subprocess.test.ts:89) fails with 'expected false to be true' for outcome.becameServiceable on hardcoded PORT=34572. Consistent, not flaky. Full serial result: 1 failed | 53 passed | 1 skipped files; 1 failed | 662 passed | 17 skipped tests. Excluded from all T12 review verdicts as pre-existing infra.

WHAT THE TEST DOES: guarded by describe.skipIf(!hasBuild) where hasBuild = existsSync('.next/standalone/server.js'). So it only RUNS after a standalone build (e.g. after our 'rm -rf web/.next' fresh Playwright builds) — before a build it is SKIPPED, which is why it surfaces now. The valid case spawns the real standalone server (env BACKEND_API_URL=http://127.0.0.1:9, PUBLIC_ORIGIN=http://localhost:34572, PORT=34572), polls tryConnect(34572) every 200ms for 8s, and asserts it became connectable.

DISCREPANCY WITH nursing-sheduler-7go: 7go states this test 'passes serial / --no-file-parallelism' and attributes flake to DEFAULT-parallelism worker port contention. But here it fails EVEN under --no-file-parallelism (serial). So 7go's root-cause assumption does not fully hold in this environment; reconcile the two (this may be a second, distinct cause or 7go's serial-passes claim may be stale).

HYPOTHESES to check:
1. Hardcoded port 34572 is held/occupied by a long-running process in the Traycer/dev host (review agents' leading hypothesis) — but note: if a listener were ACCEPTING on 34572, tryConnect would succeed and the test would spuriously PASS; becameServiceable=false instead implies the spawned server never served (crashed/exited or a non-accepting occupant). Verify what actually holds 34572 (ss -ltnp | grep 34572) and whether the child exits early.
2. The spawned standalone server crashes on startup in this env for an unrelated reason (standalone artifact staleness after the rm -rf .next + rebuild dance; Node version; missing runtime env; next.config output:'standalone' assumptions) — capture child stdio (currently stdio:'ignore') and exitCode to see.
3. 8s timeout too short under a loaded host.
4. next.config output mode: confirm standalone output is actually produced/consistent with what the test spawns.

REPRO: rm -rf web/.next; build (Playwright webServer or next build) so .next/standalone/server.js exists; corepack pnpm -C web exec vitest run instrumentation.subprocess --no-file-parallelism.

FIX DIRECTION (likely shared with 7go): ephemeral/randomized port helper keeping PORT + PUBLIC_ORIGIN in sync; capture child stdio on failure; assert fail-fast behavior unchanged. Coordinate with / possibly supersede parts of nursing-sheduler-7go. Test-only; no product impact.

title: Consider: bare-duration (hours-only) shift authoring for contracted-hours accounting
description:
OPEN QUESTION / think-about item surfaced during T09 (People & Shift Types editors) cold re-review Major 7.

DL10-D4 settled that T09's working-time editor is clock-<select>-only (start/end + rest → auto-derived read-only duration); the design prototype overrides spec 03 FR-ED-06a for authoring, and T09 will NOT expose a bare-duration authoring mode. That is correct for ordinary FLOOR shifts, where the clock in/out + rest fully define the shift and the duration is a consequence.

BUT the core validator + producer schema DO accept a valid bare '{durationMinutes: N}' shape (clocks absent). The one place that shape is genuinely meaningful is HOURS-ONLY accounting toward contracted hours — e.g. a flexible/training/on-call block that contributes N hours toward a person's contracted-hours target without a fixed clock window. This is a T12 (Card editors + Contracted Hours, nursing-sheduler-qq0.12) / DL09 (minute-unit + working-time coefficients) concern, NOT a floor-shift working-time concern.

THINK ABOUT (defer decision to T12): Does the contracted-hours model need users to author duration-only shift types (no clocks)? If yes, where does that authoring live — a dedicated contracted-hours/coefficient surface (T12), NOT the T09 floor-shift working-time editor? If such shapes can arise from import/backend, T09 already preserves them on unrelated edits (DL10-D4 data-preservation guardrail) — confirm that guardrail is sufficient vs. needing real authoring.

No action now. Revisit when T12 is planned/implemented. Cross-refs: DL10-D4 (decision-logs/10-design-review-product-decisions), T09 cold-review Major 7, spec 03 FR-ED-06a, spec 12 contracted-hours, DL09.

title: Mobile person×date matrix — density strategy + touch paint (accepted-suboptimal, deferred)
description:
Design-completeness gap from the parity/feasibility review (findings 1-2). DECISION (2026-07-15): proceed with the current non-optimal design for the parity build; defer the improvement. Two issues on the Shift Requests person×date matrix — the handover's flagged KEY mobile challenge (handover section 8):

(1) Density = horizontal-scroll-only. The matrix answers ~100 people × ~31 days with a nested overflow:auto pane + sticky first column (shrink-a-table), not the progressive-disclosure / focused-row strategy the handover asked for. On a phone the 150px sticky nurse column eats ~40% of the viewport (~5-6 day cells visible); body scrolls ~3400px inside a 68vh pane; nested scroll fights page scroll. (ScreenRequests 90,94,523,536.)

(2) Quick-paint bulk entry is mouse-only (mousedown/mouseenter/global mouseup; no touch/pointer events) — the bulk-fill gesture is dead on touch, the priority device class. Single-cell tap-to-edit still works, so it degrades to one-cell-at-a-time. (ScreenRequests 347-350, 531-532.)

Revisit trigger: after the parity build ships / before any real mobile launch. Likely needs a design-track loop for a genuine mobile strategy (focused-row/progressive-disclosure view + touch-capable paint), not a mechanical patch.
design: Not a build-time patch — wants a real mobile matrix strategy from the design track (focused-row / progressive-disclosure view, virtualization, date-window paging) plus touch/pointer-event paint. Parity build proceeds with scroll-only + mouse paint as an accepted known limitation.
acceptance_criteria: The person×date matrix is usable on a phone for ~100 people × a month: bulk entry works on touch, and density is handled by a deliberate mobile strategy rather than horizontal scroll alone.
